intitule: Resource Exercise: Identifying Question Tags and Wh-Questions

enonce.texte: Instructions: Read each sentence carefully. Then, identify whether the underlined part is a Question Tag or a Wh-question word. Finally, complete the missing tag or wh-word where necessary.

questions.texte: You are a Cameroonian citizen, <u>aren't you</u>?
<u>What</u> is the capital city of Cameroon?
She didn't attend the civic education seminar, __________ ?

enonce.texte: Instructions: Match each statement in Column A with the correct question tag in Column B. Write the letter of your answer.

questions.texte: Column A: He plays football every weekend.
Column A: They are not from the same ethnic group.
Column A: You have lived in Douala before.
Column A: The president will announce the new law.
Column A: I am a responsible citizen.

intitule: Competence Exercise: Using Wh-Questions in a Civic Context

enonce.texte:
Context: You are a young reporter for your school newspaper. You have to interview the mayor of your local council about the upcoming elections.

Instructions: Formulate five (5) correct Wh-questions you would ask the mayor. Use at least three different Wh-words (Who, What, Where, When, Why, How, How many, etc.).

questions.reponse: When will the local elections take place?
How many candidates are running for the position of mayor?
Why is it important for young people to vote?
What are the main responsibilities of an elected council member?
Where can citizens go to check their voter registration?

intitule: Competence Exercise: Real-life Problem Solving with Question Tags

enonce.texte:
Problem Situation: Your friend, Paul, is spreading a false rumor that voting is not important for people under 30. You need to convince him using polite but firm questions.

Instructions: Write a short dialogue (4 exchanges minimum) where you use at least three (3) different question tags to check Paul's understanding and challenge his false ideas. Explain why your tags are appropriate.

questions.reponse:
You: Paul, you know that everyone's vote counts in a democracy, doesn't it?
Paul: I don't think so. Young people's votes don't change anything.
You: But you participated in the school council elections last year, didn't you?
Paul: Yes, but that's different.
You: So, you haven't read the article about the 2018 election decided by a small margin, have you?
Paul: No, I haven't.
You: Then let's read it together. It shows that every single vote matters, doesn't it?

sousQuestions.texte: Explain why you used 'doesn't it' in the first sentence.
sousQuestions.reponse: I used 'doesn't it' because the main verb 'counts' is in the present simple positive form. The statement is a general truth about democracy, and the tag expects agreement from Paul.

sousQuestions.texte: Explain why you used 'didn't you' in the third sentence.
sousQuestions.reponse: I used 'didn't you' because the main verb 'participated' is in the past simple positive form. The tag is negative and refers to a specific past action that Paul experienced.

intitule: Resource Exercise: Transforming Statements into Questions

enonce.texte: Instructions: Transform the following statements into two different types of questions: (a) a Wh-question and (b) a sentence with a question tag. The first one has been done as an example.

questions.texte: Statement: The government built a new hospital in Bafoussam.
questions.reponse: a) What did the government build in Bafoussam? b) The government built a new hospital in Bafoussam, didn't it?

questions.texte: Statement: Citizens must respect the national anthem.
questions.reponse: a) What must citizens respect? OR Why must citizens respect the national anthem? b) Citizens must respect the national anthem, mustn't they?

questions.texte: Statement: The opposition party won three seats in the last election.
questions.reponse: a) How many seats did the opposition party win in the last election? b) The opposition party won three seats in the last election, didn't it?
